topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed, casting a pale, sickly light across Liam’s face. He hadn't meant to look, but curiosity, sharp and unwelcome, had dragged him to the open document. The title, elegantly scripted in a flowing calligraphy font, read: *The Unseen Muse*. His name, in bold italics, dominated the first chapter. A knot tightened in his chest, a cold, heavy pressure that made his breath catch. He scrolled down, his fingers trembling slightly as they tracked the narrative. Every description of his mundane habits—the way he brewed his coffee, the precise angle of his frown when concentrating—was rendered with an intimacy that felt invasive. He closed the laptop with a soft, decisive snap, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet apartment. He stood by the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a sudden, dizzying need to retreat, to disappear into the anonymity of the night.

[story 2]

Maya found the file tucked away in a rarely used folder on her partner’s desktop. The file name was deceptively innocuous, but the moment she clicked it, the carefully constructed composure she’d maintained all evening began to fray. Pages upon pages detailed shared memories, whispered inside jokes, and intimate moments she hadn't even realized she’d shared so deeply. A flush crept up her neck, warm and undeniable. She reread a passage where he described her laughter—a sound she hadn't consciously realized she’d made that particular way. Her hands started to shake, the fine motor control dissolving. She backed away from the screen, stumbling slightly against the bookshelf. "What is this?" she managed, her voice thin and reedy. The silence that followed was thick, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of her own pulse against her ribs.

[story 3]

Elias felt a sudden, crushing weight settle onto his shoulders. He’d been tidying up the study when he happened upon the manuscript. It was a sprawling, meticulous account of his life, written in a voice that was startlingly perceptive, almost painfully accurate. The sheer volume of his existence cataloged, analyzed, and transformed into prose was too much to process. He walked to the kitchen, needing the grounding reality of the mundane. He poured a glass of water, his movements stiff. Every sentence seemed to pull him deeper into the text, each observation a tiny, sharp pinprick against his skin. He gripped the glass so tightly his knuckles turned white, wishing he could simply close his eyes and let the pressure dissipate, wishing the paper and ink would vanish.
